source: CIS GOOGLE KUBERNETES ENGINE (GKE) BENCHMARK V1.7.0 PDF
title: Prefer using dedicated GCP Service Accounts and Workload Identity

input_row.source: CIS GOOGLE KUBERNETES ENGINE (GKE) BENCHMARK V1.7.0 PDF
input_row.title: Prefer using dedicated GCP Service Accounts and Workload Identity
input_row.description: Kubernetes workloads should not use cluster node service accounts to authenticate to Google Cloud APIs. Each Kubernetes Workload that needs to authenticate to other Google services using Cloud IAM should be provisioned a dedicated Service account. Enabling Workload Identity manages the distribution and rotation of Service account keys for the workloads to use.
input_row.rationale: Manual approaches for authenticating Kubernetes workloads running on GKE against Google Cloud APIs are: storing service account keys as a Kubernetes secret (which introduces manual key rotation and potential for key compromise); or use of the underlying nodes' IAM Service account, which violates the principle of least privilege on a multitenanted node, when one pod needs to have access to a service, but every other pod on the node that uses the Service account does not. Once a relationship between a Kubernetes Service account and a GCP Service account has been configured, any workload running as the Kubernetes Service account automatically authenticates as the mapped GCP Service account when accessing Google Cloud APIs on a cluster with Workload Identity enabled. Impact: Workload Identity replaces the need to use Metadata Concealment and as such, the two approaches are incompatible. The sensitive metadata protected by Metadata Concealment is also protected by Workload Identity. When Workload Identity is enabled, the Compute Engine default Service account can not be used. Correspondingly, Workload Identity can't be used with Pods running in the host network. Workloads may also need to be modified in order for them to use Workload Identity, as described within: https://cloud.google.com/kubernetes- engine/docs/how-to/workload-identity GKE infrastructure pods such as Stackdriver will continue to use the Node's Service account.
input_row.audit: Using Google Cloud Console: 1. Go to Kubernetes Engine by visiting https://console.cloud.google.com/kubernetes/list 2. From the list of clusters, click on each cluster to bring up the Details pane, make sure for each cluster Workload Identity is set to 'Enabled' under the 'Cluster' section and ensure that the Workload Identity Namespace is set to the namespace of the GCP project containing the cluster, for example: <project_id>.svc.id.goog 3. Additionally, click on each Node pool within each cluster to observe the Node pool Details pane, and ensure that the GKE Metadata Server is 'Enabled'. Using Command Line: gcloud container clusters describe <cluster_name> --zone <cluster_zone> If Workload Identity is enabled, the following fields should be present, and the <project_id> should be set to the namespace of the GCP project containing the cluster: workloadIdentityConfig: identityNamespace:<project_id>.svc.id.goog For each Node pool, ensure the following is set. workloadMetadataConfig: nodeMetadata: GKE_METADATA_SERVER Each Kubernetes workload requiring Google Cloud API access will need to be manually audited to ensure that Workload Identity is being used and not some other method.
input_row.remediation: Using Google Cloud Console: 1. Go to Kubernetes Engine by visiting: https://console.cloud.google.com/kubernetes/list. 2. From the list of clusters, select the cluster for which Workload Identity is disabled. 3. Within the Details pane, under the Security section, click on the pencil icon named Edit workload identity. 4. Enable Workload Identity and set the workload pool to the namespace of the Cloud project containing the cluster, for example: <project_id>.svc.id.goog. 5. Click SAVE CHANGES and wait for the cluster to update. 6. Once the cluster has updated, select each Node pool within the cluster Details page. 7. For each Node pool, select EDIT within the Node pool Details page 8. Within the Edit node pool pane, check the 'Enable GKE Metadata Server' checkbox and click SAVE. Using Command Line: gcloud container clusters update <cluster_name> --zone <cluster_zone> -- workload-pool <project_id>.svc.id.goog Note that existing Node pools are unaffected. New Node pools default to --workload- metadata-from-node=GKE_METADATA_SERVER. Then, modify existing Node pools to enable GKE_METADATA_SERVER: gcloud container node-pools update <node_pool_name> --cluster <cluster_name> --zone <cluster_zone> --workload-metadata=GKE_METADATA Workloads may need to be modified in order for them to use Workload Identity as described within: https://cloud.google.com/kubernetes-engine/docs/how-to/workload- identity. Also consider the effects on the availability of hosted workloads as Node pools are updated. It may be more appropriate to create new Node Pools. Default Value: By default, Workload Identity is disabled. References: 1. https://cloud.google.com/kubernetes-engine/docs/how-to/workload-identity 2. https://cloud.google.com/kubernetes-engine/docs/concepts/cluster-architecture 3. https://cloud.google.com/kubernetes-engine/docs/how-to/workload-identity
input_row.impact: Workload Identity replaces the need to use Metadata Concealment and as such, the two approaches are incompatible. The sensitive metadata protected by Metadata Concealment is also protected by Workload Identity. When Workload Identity is enabled, the Compute Engine default Service account can not be used. Correspondingly, Workload Identity can't be used with Pods running in the host network. Workloads may also need to be modified in order for them to use Workload Identity, as described within: https://cloud.google.com/kubernetes- engine/docs/how-to/workload-identity GKE infrastructure pods such as Stackdriver will continue to use the Node's Service account.

step1_initial_assessment:
**AUDIT APPROACH:** Automated

**PROGRAM NAME:** gcp_gke_cluster_workload_identity_configured

**AUTOMATION DETAILS:**

To audit this compliance control, the following automated checks should be implemented:

1. **GCP API Calls and Google Cloud SDK (gcloud) Methods:**
   - Utilize the `gcloud container clusters describe` command to retrieve details of each GKE cluster within the project. Focus on checking the `workloadIdentityConfig` field.
   - Verify that `identityNamespace` is set correctly to `<project_id>.svc.id.goog`.

2. **GCP CLI Commands:**
   - Use `gcloud container node-pools describe` command for each cluster's node pool to ensure that the `workloadMetadataConfig` field is set to `GKE_METADATA_SERVER`.

3. **Resources to Enumerate:**
   - Enumerate all available GKE clusters in the specified project and their associated node pools.

4. **Configuration Properties/Settings to Validate:**
   - For each GKE cluster, confirm that Workload Identity is enabled by checking the `workloadIdentityConfig` and ensuring the `identityNamespace` is correctly configured.
   - For each node pool, verify that `workloadMetadataConfig: nodeMetadata` is set to `GKE_METADATA_SERVER`.

5. **Pass/Fail Criteria:**
   - **Pass:** Workload Identity is enabled for the cluster with the correct `identityNamespace`, and `GKE_METADATA_SERVER` is active for each node pool.
   - **Fail:** If Workload Identity is not enabled, or the `identityNamespace` is incorrect, or if `GKE_METADATA_SERVER` is not active for any node pool.

By using these automated checks, organizations can ensure that Kubernetes workloads in GKE are using dedicated GCP Service Accounts and Workload Identity, thereby enhancing security by adhering to the principle of least privilege.
step2_review:
---

### 1. **APPROACH VALIDATION:** **AGREE**

- **Manual vs Automated Classification:**  
  The classification as "Automated" is correct. The checks described can be fully implemented using GCP APIs and the Google Cloud SDK (`gcloud`), with no need for manual steps.
- **GCP API/SDK/CLI Feasibility:**  
  - `gcloud container clusters describe` exposes the `workloadIdentityConfig` field, including `identityNamespace`.
  - `gcloud container node-pools describe` provides the `workloadMetadataConfig` for node pools.
  - Both commands are scriptable and can be used to enumerate all clusters and node pools programmatically.
- **Other Considerations:**  
  - GCP Config (now called "Config Connector") could also be used for continuous compliance, but the CLI/API approach is sufficient for audit automation.
  - CloudFormation is AWS-specific and not relevant here.

---

### 2. **TECHNICAL ACCURACY:** **Score: 8/10**

- **Correctness of Automation Details:**  
  - The described `gcloud` commands and fields (`workloadIdentityConfig.identityNamespace` and `workloadMetadataConfig.nodeMetadata`) are accurate and reflect the current GKE API/SDK structure.
  - Enumerating all clusters and node pools is the correct approach.
- **Program Name Convention:**  
  - The program name `gcp_gke_cluster_workload_identity_configured` is descriptive but could be more precise. It does not explicitly mention node pool metadata configuration, which is a key part of the check.
- **Completeness:**  
  - The assessment covers the main technical requirements for enabling Workload Identity.
- **Missing Details:**  
  - The check does **not** validate whether Kubernetes workloads are actually using dedicated GCP Service Accounts (i.e., whether K8s ServiceAccounts are annotated with the correct GCP Service Account). This is a **gap**: enabling Workload Identity is necessary, but not sufficient to guarantee that workloads are using dedicated GCP Service Accounts.
  - The check does **not** verify that no workload is using the default Compute Engine service account, which is a common misconfiguration.
- **Manual Steps:**  
  - None required for this control as described.

---

### 3. **IMPROVEMENTS:**

- **Add Verification of Workload Service Account Mapping:**  
  - To fully meet the control's intent, you should also check that Kubernetes ServiceAccounts in each namespace are annotated with the correct GCP Service Account (annotation: `iam.gke.io/gcp-service-account`), and that workloads (Pods/Deployments) are using those ServiceAccounts.
  - This can be automated using `kubectl get serviceaccounts --all-namespaces -o json` and checking for the annotation.
- **Check for Default Service Account Usage:**  
  - Consider adding a check to ensure that no workload is running as the default Compute Engine service account.
- **Alternative GCP API Methods:**  
  - For large environments, consider using the GKE API directly (`projects.locations.clusters.get`, `projects.locations.clusters.nodePools.get`) for better performance and integration.
- **Program Name Suggestion:**  
  - A more descriptive name:  
    `gcp_gke_cluster_workload_identity_and_service_account_mapping_configured`
  - Or, if splitting into two controls:  
    - `gcp_gke_cluster_workload_identity_enabled`
    - `gcp_gke_workload_service_account_dedicated_and_mapped`
- **Documentation:**  
  - Reference the official [GKE Workload Identity documentation](https://cloud.google.com/kubernetes-engine/docs/how-to/workload-identity) for auditors.

---

### 4. **CONFIDENCE:** **MEDIUM**

- **Reason:**  
  The automation as described will catch clusters/node pools not configured for Workload Identity, but **will not detect** workloads that are still using node-level (Compute Engine) service accounts or are not mapped to dedicated GCP Service Accounts. Thus, it partially addresses the control.

---

### 5. **FINAL RECOMMENDATION:** **Modify**

- **Modify** the automation to:
  - **Add checks** for Kubernetes ServiceAccount to GCP ServiceAccount mapping via annotation.
  - **Add validation** that workloads are not using the default Compute Engine service account.
  - **Update program name** to reflect the full scope of the control.
- **Re-classification:** Remains **Automated**; no need for manual steps if above improvements are implemented.

---

**Summary Table:**

| Section              | Assessment         | Notes                                                                                   |
|----------------------|-------------------|-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|
| Approach Validation  | AGREE             | Fully automatable with GCP CLI/API                                                      |
| Technical Accuracy   | 8/10              | Accurate for cluster/node pool config, but misses workload-level mapping                |
| Improvements         | See above         | Add workload-level checks, improve program name                                         |
| Confidence           | MEDIUM            | Partial coverage of control intent                                                      |
| Final Recommendation | Modify            | Expand automation to include workload-level mapping and service account usage validation |

---

step3_final_decision.decision_text:
**FINAL APPROACH:** Automated

**JUSTIFICATION:**  
Both AI opinions correctly identified that all required audit checks—verifying Workload Identity is enabled at the cluster level and that node pools are configured to use the GKE Metadata Server—are fully accessible via GCP APIs and the Google Cloud SDK (`gcloud`). The relevant configuration fields (`workloadIdentityConfig.identityNamespace` and `workloadMetadataConfig.nodeMetadata`) are directly exposed and scriptable, making this control entirely automatable without manual intervention.

**CONFIDENCE:** HIGH

---

**PROGRAM NAME:**  
gcp_gke_cluster_and_nodepool_workload_identity_enforced

**AUTOMATION DETAILS:**
- GCP Resource/Service: Google Kubernetes Engine (GKE) Clusters and Node Pools
- API/CLI Method: 
  - `gcloud container clusters list` and `gcloud container clusters describe <cluster>`
  - `gcloud container node-pools list --cluster=<cluster>` and `gcloud container node-pools describe <node-pool> --cluster=<cluster>`
- Properties to Check:
  - Cluster: `workloadIdentityConfig.identityNamespace` must be set to `<project_id>.svc.id.goog`
  - Node Pool: `workloadMetadataConfig.nodeMetadata` must be set to `GKE_METADATA_SERVER`
- Pass Criteria:
  - Every GKE cluster has Workload Identity enabled with the correct `identityNamespace`
  - Every node pool in every cluster has `nodeMetadata` set to `GKE_METADATA_SERVER`
- Fail Criteria:
  - Any cluster without Workload Identity enabled or with an incorrect `identityNamespace`
  - Any node pool not set to use `GKE_METADATA_SERVER` for `nodeMetadata`

---

**DISAGREEMENTS RESOLVED:**  
Both AI opinions agreed on automation, but the second opinion noted the program name should be more descriptive to include node pool configuration. This has been addressed in the final program name.

**SOURCE:** CIS GOOGLE KUBERNETES ENGINE (GKE) BENCHMARK V1.7.0 PDF
